title: The transformer paper-recommendation advantage is concentrated at the head of the impact distribution and decays to null across the broad literature: a 4-checkpoint, all-26-field convergence study of SPECTER vs TF-IDF
description: A companion single-subfield study found that SPECTER (a paper-trained transformer) beat a TF-IDF baseline at held-out citation prediction in High-Energy Physics by +15.4% relative MAP (paired-bootstrap p = 0.0005) — measured on the citation-dense, top-cited slice of one subfield. Here we test whether that advantage generalizes, and how it depends on how deep into the literature one looks. Using a checkpointed, resumable producer/consumer pipeline, we pull an impact-ranked OpenAlex corpus (most-cited first) across all 26 top-level fields, build a complete in-corpus citation graph and PageRank per field, embed title+abstract with SPECTER on a local AMD GPU (ROCm, ~9.1 docs/s), and run the identical held-out citation-prediction evaluation (SPECTER vs TF-IDF vs word2vec vs a text-free graph recommender; mask 30% of in-corpus references; Recall@k / MAP / MRR with bootstrap 95% CIs and a paired test) in every field — then grow the corpus and re-measure across four scales (78,000 → 130,000 → 361,800 works). The result is a clean convergence finding: at the head of the impact distribution (checkpoint 1, 78k works) SPECTER beats TF-IDF in 16/26 fields with a large, nearly significant across-field edge (combined mean ΔMAP +0.0095, 95% CI [-0.0005, +0.0195], p = 0.062); as the impact-ranked corpus broadens the edge decays monotonically to null (12/26 then 11/26 wins; combined ΔMAP -0.0008 then -0.0019, 95% CI [-0.0075, +0.0034], p = 0.49). Checkpoint 4 (50k/field target) hit the corpus availability + rate-limit ceiling at 361,800 works and reproduced checkpoint 3 exactly — the result is converged. The edge survives where fine-grained phrase meaning carries relevance (Computer Science, Social Sciences, Neuroscience, Biochemistry) and reverses in physical-science / pharmacology fields where exact-term matching wins (Pharmacology -0.040, Chemistry -0.025, Earth & Planetary -0.020, all p < 0.001); Physics & Astronomy — the field containing the HEP slice — flips to a significant loss under broad-literature aggregation. Citation-count Gini ranges 0.243-0.501 and interdisciplinarity 0.169-0.558 across fields but neither predicts the split. Headline: neural paper-recommendation's edge is concentrated in the head of the impact distribution; across the broad literature it is not a general win. Limitations (50k/field corpus plateau, single embedding model, in-corpus vs global citations, closed-world citation-density-selected eval) are stated plainly, and the result is converged (ckpt3 == ckpt4). MIT (code) / CC-BY-4.0 (data, via OpenAlex CC0). A DOI will be minted on the next research-atlas release.
